title: A Dollar a Day
summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the University of Southern Mississippi in December 2015, Eddie Holloway, born in Hattiesburg in 1952, introduces his parents, his mother Beatrice, a beautician who loved to fish, and his father Willie, who delivered fertilizer by the barrel at the Meridian Fertilizer Company for a dollar a day plus a dollar bet that he could not last.

title: That's My Word
summary: Holloway describes his father's more than sixty years at the one company, rising to office custodian and then watchman, a man who would not sign a contract because his word was his bond and whom Holloway still calls the best person he ever met, remembered across Hattiesburg for that character.

title: A Self-Contained Street
summary: Holloway paints Mobile Street, where the family lived at 904 with his mother's Bea's Beauty Salon attached, as a self-contained Black community of restaurants, salons and barbershops, a physician, a dentist, a pharmacist, churches, cleaners, and motels, where the professionals of Black Hattiesburg lived and where nearly every need was met.

title: A Thirty-Mile Catchment
summary: He recalls sitting Saturdays with Miss Thelma Williams at the cleaners, and a Mobile Street so busy on Saturdays, drawing people from a thirty-mile radius around towns like McLain and Wiggins, that you had to step into the street to pass, in an era when nearby Camp Shelby was largely a summer-training facility rather than the wartime base of a hundred thousand men.

title: Schools Built to Stall Integration
summary: Holloway traces his schooling from the Maddie Robinson kindergarten through W.H. Jones Elementary, which opened in 1958, to Rowan High, and recalls overhearing educators, including the Mississippi Teachers Association leader across the street, explain that schools like Jones were built across the state to stall full integration, with separate associations and meeting days for Black and white teachers.

title: The Same Faces in Different Places
summary: After his mother died when he was eleven, Holloway grew even closer to the teachers who were also members of his church and his Sunday school superintendents, the same faces in church and classroom who knew how he was doing in both, an anchored community that filled whatever educational voids existed at home.

title: I Wanted to Be Informed
summary: Holloway describes a hunger less for schooling than to be informed, knowing there were important things he did not know, and watching the way the teachers and principals dressed, walked, and carried themselves, deciding the path to that life was to become like them.

title: The Cardboard Table
summary: Holloway recalls his father, schooled only in what he called the Little Red Building and calling a zero an ought, setting up a cardboard table on the porch each day so his son did his lessons before going out, a struggle that made the boy think him old-fashioned, yet never lessened the love he still feels for the best person he knew.

title: He's on a Bicycle
summary: Holloway treasures his father's quiet wisdom, recounting how, when a man on a bicycle mocked his new Chevrolet as a poor man's car, his father gently shamed him by noting the critic was on a bicycle, the kind of lesson in silence, never a gruff or profane word, that he still draws on in any hard moment.

title: They Lived and Breathed Civil Rights
summary: Holloway describes how, after his mother's death, an aunt from Alabama took him to live with her family near Birmingham, wise but unschooled people who lived and breathed civil rights, loved Martin Luther King, and instilled in him a heaping dose of pride, taking him to the steel mills on payday to see the workers and the waiting mothers.

title: Iron the Paper Bags
summary: Holloway recounts the relatives' charge that he uphold their standards, save, work, and never beg, and their extreme meticulousness, multiple baths a day, ironed handkerchiefs and even ironed paper lunch bags so no one would think a bag reused, a strictness that wore on the boy but whose intentions he never lost sight of.

title: Red Socks
summary: Holloway smiles over the bright-colored clothes the relatives mailed him from catalogs, the red and yellow socks he refused to wear, and the steady stream of letters, packages, and calls of love that assured him he had everlasting kin; fiery about civil rights, they gave money and bonded people out of jail though they did not march.

title: The Sixteenth Street Church
summary: Holloway remembers being taken to the Sixteenth Street Baptist Church and told of its bombing, and to Willie Mays's Say Hey restaurant and the Black Barons' stadium, too young then to fully grasp it but absorbing the relatives' daily charge to ask the church for wisdom and knowledge because he would not have them once he returned to Hattiesburg.

title: Never Let Anyone Talk Against Me
summary: Holloway senses, from his father's repeated plea never to let anyone turn him against him, that the Alabama relatives may have wished to take him, a thing he could never confirm and prayed his father would never have to carry, even as he honors a father who treated him, he says, like the best person ever made.

title: Get Your Lesson
summary: Holloway describes believing, because the teachers, his Alabama kin, his father, and the porch people who called out as he passed all told him so, that education was the path to economic and personal freedom, and recalls an elder telling him there was a reason people were killed trying to get an education: something in it would set him free.

title: Equal Footing, Not Integration
summary: Holloway came to understand that Mississippi funded new Black schools as a bargaining point to forestall desegregation, and that the old stalwarts of Black education sought not integration but equal footing, equal attendance money, new books, and lab equipment to match the white schools, recalling a lone new geometry textbook that finally arrived in late 1968.

title: A Visionary Before His Time
summary: Holloway calls the educator N.R. Burger a visionary who deliberately did graduate work at Cornell to stand on par with the white superintendent and built a separate but stellar Hattiesburg school system, in one of the few places in the state offering a twelfth grade for Black students, drawing families from fifty miles to feed graduates on to Alcorn State.

title: I'm Paving a Way for You
summary: Holloway recounts that his father never voted and could not be moved to, valuing his relationships with whites of power and saying he was paving a way for his son, a way Holloway felt downtown, where his father's accounts and standing spared him the layers of screening other Black customers faced at the stores and pharmacies.

title: Count Them One by One
summary: Holloway describes picking up snippets in his mother's beauty shop, where civil rights workers courted beauticians, and learning of voting only in the ninth or tenth grade from teachers like N.R. Burger; the registrar Theron Lynd was talked of all over the city, the subject of the book Count Them One by One, after a group of more than fifteen had tried and failed to register.

title: The Air-Conditioned Room
summary: Holloway accepted that Black people could not vote and that facilities were separate, but never that it was permanent, and locates an early consciousness of race at a student-council meeting at the university where he was the only African American, in a cool air-conditioned room he vowed one day to have, drawing on a lifelong gift for reframing any situation toward possibility.

title: My Mother's Whistle
summary: Holloway names the marches and the nearly nightly mass meetings, moving from True Light to St. Paul to Mount Zion to Palmer's Crossing, as the first civil rights activity he remembers, attending more freely only after his mother, whose whistle from the back porch had once carried three blocks to call him home, had died, and when relatives visited from Muskegon.

title: They Came to Set Us Free
summary: Holloway describes his first integrated meetings as reflecting hope and freedom, likening conditioned acceptance to a chained elephant that comes to be held by a string, and electrified that the freedom riders had come to set them free; his father, for all his hesitancy, never set limits on him, and once asked the white office manager whether his son should transfer to Hattiesburg High.

title: The Right Decision for Eddie
summary: Told to stay where he was, Holloway came to see Rowan High as pride itself, the place that gave him the teach-a-man-to-fish wisdom he would not have gotten at Hattiesburg High, where many who transferred under freedom of choice returned or struggled, and which, for whatever reason, those transfers still attend his Rowan reunions.

title: I Never Experienced That
summary: Holloway reflects that parents who sent children to the white school thought the grass greener and the resources better, even amid harassment he heard of but never himself experienced, and notes he became the first student of color as a resident assistant and officer assistant and the first to staff the upscale Elam Arms residence.

title: To Be Near My Dad
summary: Holloway recalls washing dishes on the campus as a high schooler and, though his mentors wanted him at Jackson State or Alcorn, choosing the University of Southern Mississippi in 1970 to stay near his widowed, remarried father; independent, he took a bus to apply and sit the ACT, and helped move in some of the first Black women students.

title: An All-White Floor
summary: Holloway describes being moved to an all-white floor as a test of whether he could relate to white students before being made, without pay that first year, a resident assistant, the men there becoming his best friends and the next year bringing the offer to be head resident of the whole hall, a stepping stone he knew his day would come.

title: Go Get Me a Couple of Books
summary: Holloway recalls the Freedom Library, called the Black Folks Library, and the Freedom School efforts at True Light Baptist Church, which he used to borrow books and attend meetings without fully joining, because his Alabama kin had instilled a pride that he buy his own belongings and not appear to be among the impoverished children driven there for food.

title: When I Sensed Danger I Would Leave
summary: Holloway describes marching but leaving when he sensed danger, as on a night uptown when he saw Klan-like men with guns and others with broken bottles, calling himself not an outlier but a lifelong independent thinker, who canvassed door to door asking people whether they had registered to vote.

title: I'd Like the Front Door
summary: Holloway recounts challenging the back-door treatment at the office of the physician Dr. A.T. Tatum by calling the federal Office of Civil Rights, which led Tatum, who had held his dying grandmother and known his father, to bar him; he became a quiet civil rights informant for twenty-five years, troubled that his own father and teachers still went to Tatum, and once told the doctor he shared a class and the same door with his daughter Nancy.

title: You Accommodated Me
summary: Holloway recalls keeping master keys at the residence-hall desk and sleeping in different rooms in fear, and a scholarship interview in Jackson where, asked his opinion of Senator James Eastland, he praised the senator's service, and the interviewer denied him the scholarship for accommodating rather than telling the truth about Eastland's farm subsidies, a lesson that taught him to frame and reframe and still say his piece.

title: A Delicate Walk
summary: Holloway describes N.R. Burger's delicate walk, required to stay on the inside while conscious of what mattered outside, and the unresolved debate over whether Burger had a role in the effort around Clyde Kennard, including a theory he has chased that a junior college was financed to keep Kennard and integration out, leaving a stellar separate system as Burger's accomplishment.

title: One Vote From Closing
summary: Holloway relays what the registrar Aubrey Lucas told him over thirty years, that Burger spoke directly with legislators and the wealthy, and that USM president William D. McCain held Mississippi higher education should be desegregated but starting at Ole Miss, since Southern stood one vote from being closed, so a disturbance over Kennard could have been the excuse to shut it down.

title: Mrs. Jean and the NAACP
summary: Holloway remembers Clyde Kennard talked of in the beauty parlor as the man who tried to attend Southern, when the only Black presence on campus he knew was a peanut seller, and credits Mobile Street figures like Mrs. Jean and the AME church's NAACP lead, and his teachers Daisy Harris Conner and Clarence McGee, while gently noting that some elders' devotion to Alcorn above all could limit growth.

title: Mississippi's Fourth HBCU
summary: Holloway shares the inside joke that USM is Mississippi's fourth historically Black university, not for open admission but for the work it does to ensure access and to serve students where they are, drawing first-generation students from a roughly hundred-twenty-mile catchment rather than the out-of-state recruits other schools chase.

title: You Got Them Here
summary: Holloway recounts a career of building services so students could get from here to there, an office of inclusion, civility and at-risk training, and round-the-clock student support, with a detour running a high-risk students program at Cumberland and four addiction-treatment clinics before returning in 1979; the African American share at USM now approaches forty percent.

title: Cutting the Sharp Edges
summary: Holloway credits a different commitment at Southern, made five years after its first two Black students, to people who cut the sharp edges off, N.R. Burger, William Carey's president J. Ralph Noonkester, who integrated the first private college in the state, and the murder of NAACP leader Vernon Dahmer, whose firebombing he sees as a wake-up call that made even the white community feel things had gone too far.

part: The Spirit, and the Inside Work
title: Build a Bridge
summary: Holloway describes serving on the Mississippi Civil Rights Commission that builds civil rights into the school curriculum with the William Winter Institute and Susan Glisson, and on the coalition against sexual assault, and bringing home the Leadership Mississippi model to found Leadership Hattiesburg, working always to build bridges that may bear someone else across tomorrow.

title: Exit With the Dream
summary: Holloway explains his hope, having seen the early plans and the hired architects for the Mississippi history and civil rights museums, that visitors exit with the dream rather than the agony, so marked by the future and not the negatives that they leave inspired, the challenge he set himself in how the story is told.

title: A Voice of Freedom
summary: Holloway calls the civil rights movement a breathtaking chance to see tomorrow from where they stood, the local strength linked to outside voices of freedom that, coming from a wealth of places, let the work move faster, and that forced Mississippi and its neighbors to see how far behind they were in equality and the educational and economic advancement the least of their people needed.

title: The Spirit
summary: Holloway describes the spirit, the unknown enforcers who would visit at night anyone who broke a boycott line, and frames it as economic steering, recalling ordering hair clippers by mail from National Bellas Hess at eleven because he could not buy them in person, and arguing that wealth is a motivator that, like the University of Missouri football team's refusal to play, can become a powerful demotivator.

title: Work From the Inside
summary: Holloway distinguishes the spirit from the Deacons of Defense, who guarded dignitaries like King, C.T. Vivian, and Shuttlesworth, and offers his own creed: that those who worked the inside of the movement, less heralded than those on the television screens, often negotiated and accomplished far more, and that he has tried to work from the inside, building bridges and open lines rather than being a silent observer.

title: They Got Them a Piece of Land
summary: Holloway recalls the Ladner sisters of Palmer's Crossing and Kelly Settlement, landowning Black communities whose strength was that they owned their property, and their movement work out of Jackson State and Tougaloo, where the white Freedom Rider Joan Trumpauer Mulholland also studied, and how Hattiesburg's teachers linked students to those who had succeeded from right there.

title: Three Animals
summary: Holloway says he was sensitive to race but never a slave to it or focused on it, seeing instead what he might share with others, and illustrates with a child who named three animals as the boogeyman, Godzilla, and a creature from the one movie at the local theater, a story he uses to show that understanding a person's world, not judging the answer, is the work.

title: Ask the Right Questions
summary: Holloway closes with a student athlete disciplined for outbursts after his grandmother, who had sent him to Southern over loftier programs, fell ill, arguing that had the authorities asked the right questions they would have understood; his life's work, he says, has been to give people the insight to ask those questions, so that USM has earned a reputation for opening its doors both ways to students from extreme circumstances.
